questions.question: Define the two main branches of the Throwable class in Java.
keyPoints: Exception class: base class for all exceptions that can be thrown by a Java program

questions.question: Compare and contrast checked and unchecked exceptions in Java with examples.
keyPoints: Checked exceptions: compiler-enforced, must be handled or declared, examples include IOException and SQLException
Unchecked exceptions: not enforced by the compiler, usually programming errors, examples include NullPointerException and ArithmeticException
Error class is also unchecked and represents JVM-level problems

questions.question: Explain the complete exception hierarchy in Java, including the Throwable class, Error branch, Exception branch, and RuntimeException branch.
keyPoints: Throwable class: root of the exception hierarchy
Error branch: represents JVM-level problems, examples include OutOfMemoryError and StackOverflowError
Exception branch: base class for all exceptions that can be thrown by a Java program, includes checked and unchecked exceptions
RuntimeException branch: subclass of Exception, represents programming errors, examples include NullPointerException and ArithmeticException
Checked vs unchecked distinction: checked exceptions are compiler-enforced, while unchecked exceptions are not

questions.question: List the key differences between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java.
keyPoints: Checked exceptions: compiler-enforced, must be handled or declared
Unchecked exceptions: not enforced by the compiler, usually programming errors

questions.question: Discuss the importance of understanding exception types in Java programming.
keyPoints: Understanding exception types is crucial for effective error handling and robust programming
Exception types help identify and handle errors in a program
Knowledge of exception types is essential for writing reliable and maintainable code
